root.narration: This is your third at-bat of the game, and you've struck out TWICE on curveballs. First at-bat: fastball-fastball-curveball, swung over the top. Second at-bat: fastball-curveball, swung over the top again. The pitcher knows you can't hit his curveball. You're stepping in for your third look. What adjustment do you make to handle the curveball this time?

choices.text: This time, I'm SITTING on the curveball. He's used it to get me out twice -- he's going back to it. I'll look for the curve and adjust to the fastball if I need to.
I need to stay back longer on every pitch. My front foot was landing too early, which is why I swing over the curveball. Stay back, keep my weight on my back leg, and let the ball come to me.
I'm just going to swing harder. If I hit the curveball hard enough, it doesn't matter that I'm on top of it. More bat speed fixes everything.

decision-curve-approach.narration: You decide to sit on the curveball. The pitcher delivers his first pitch -- it's a FASTBALL on the inner half! Because you're looking for the curveball, the fastball looks extra fast. It zips past you for strike one. Now you're 0-1, and you realize the pitcher might not throw the curve right away since he knows you're looking for it. What do you do now?

choices.text: Stay on the curveball. He's going to throw it eventually -- it's his strikeout pitch. When he throws it, I'll be ready.
OK, he's throwing fastballs. I'll switch to looking for the fastball now and forget about the curve.
With 0-1, I need to shorten my swing and be ready for BOTH pitches. Choke up, widen my stance, and put the ball in play.

outcome-stay-back-great.narration: You focus on keeping your weight back. The pitcher starts you with a fastball -- you stay back, recognize it, and foul it off. Then comes the curveball -- his go-to weapon. But this time, because you stayed back, you see the ball longer. You wait... wait... the ball breaks down and you drive it on a line to center field! Base hit! The adjustment WORKED! You stayed back, gave yourself more time to see the break, and drove the curveball instead of swinging over it!

outcome.headline: Base Hit! Curveball Conquered!
outcome.explanation: THIS is how you make an in-game adjustment. The reason you were swinging over the curveball was TIMING, not recognition. You were starting your swing too early, which meant by the time the slower curveball arrived, your bat was already past the hitting zone. By staying back -- keeping your weight on your back leg and letting the ball travel deeper -- you gave yourself extra milliseconds to see the ball break. Those milliseconds are everything against a curveball. The adjustment wasn't about swinging differently. It was about WAITING differently. Same swing, better timing. That's the key to hitting curveballs: let it come to you.
outcome.whatToRemember: When you're swinging over curveballs, the fix is to STAY BACK. Keep your weight on your back leg longer and let the ball travel deeper before committing. Same swing, better timing.

outcome-same-approach-bad.narration: You step in with the exact same approach -- see ball, hit ball, swing hard. The pitcher goes fastball-curveball AGAIN. The curveball breaks down and you swing over the top of it for the THIRD time today. Strike three. Three at-bats, three strikeouts on the same pitch. The pitcher barely had to think -- he threw the same sequence and you gave him the same result every time. Einstein said insanity is doing the same thing and expecting different results.

outcome.headline: Struck Out AGAIN! No Adjustment Made!
outcome.explanation: Swinging harder does NOT fix the problem. The reason you're swinging over the curveball isn't lack of effort -- it's timing. The curveball is slower and breaks downward. When you swing at fastball timing, your bat arrives too early and too high. Swinging HARDER just makes you even more early and even more on top. The fix is mechanical: stay back, let the ball travel, recognize the spin. More bat speed on bad timing equals louder misses, not hits. Making adjustments is one of the hardest and most important skills in hitting. The pitcher made the same adjustment he made before (throw the curve). You need to make one too.
outcome.whatToRemember: If the same pitch is getting you out, you MUST adjust. Swinging harder is not an adjustment. Change your timing, your approach, or your pitch selection. The definition of insanity: doing the same thing and expecting different results.

outcome-stay-curve-good.narration: You stay on the curveball. The pitcher throws another fastball -- strike two. Now you're 0-2. Finally, here comes the curveball. You're ready for it, you see it, and you swing -- but you're so geared up for it that you pull the ball foul. Strike three. You were ON the curveball, but being 0-2 meant you had no margin for error. You fouled the one pitch you were looking for.

outcome.headline: Foul Ball, Strike Three. Close But Not Enough.
outcome.explanation: Sitting on the curveball was a decent idea, but the execution had a problem: you let the fastball put you in an 0-2 hole before the curveball ever came. When you're looking for one specific pitch and the pitcher throws something else, you're a sitting duck on the other pitch. By the time the curveball came, you were in a two-strike desperation mode and couldn't put a quality swing on it. A better approach: stay back on EVERY pitch (which handles both fastball and curveball) instead of committing to looking for one pitch. The 'staying back' adjustment works against ALL pitches because it's about timing, not pitch selection.
outcome.whatToRemember: Sitting on one pitch can leave you vulnerable to everything else. A mechanical adjustment (staying back) works against ALL pitches, while a pitch-selection adjustment (sitting curveball) only works against one.

outcome-switch-fb-okay.narration: You switch back to looking for the fastball. The pitcher throws... a curveball. You're not ready for it, and you swing over the top -- AGAIN. Strike two. He went right back to the weapon that's been getting you out all day. Now you're 0-2 and flailing. He throws another curveball and you chase. Strike three. You kept switching your approach and never committed to a plan. The pitcher kept it simple while you complicated things.

outcome.headline: Struck Out. Kept Changing Plans.
outcome.explanation: Switching your approach pitch-to-pitch is a recipe for failure. When you go from 'look curveball' to 'look fastball' after one pitch, you're guessing, not adjusting. A real adjustment is a MECHANICAL change you apply to EVERY pitch, not a pitch-to-pitch guessing game. Staying back works against the fastball AND the curveball. Choking up works against everything. These are ADJUSTMENTS. Guessing 'curveball this time... no, fastball this time' is just coin-flipping. Have a plan and stick with it for the whole at-bat.
outcome.whatToRemember: Don't switch your approach pitch-to-pitch. That's guessing, not adjusting. A real adjustment is a mechanical change you apply consistently. Pick a plan and commit to it.

outcome-two-strike-great.narration: Smart adjustment! You choke up an inch on the bat, widen your stance slightly, and commit to putting the ball in play. The pitcher throws a curveball -- his weapon. But this time, your shortened swing and wider base keep you balanced. You poke a single through the left side! Base hit! You didn't crush it, but you adjusted your approach, shortened your swing, and put the ball in play. That's baseball IQ -- knowing when to modify your swing based on the situation and your struggles.

outcome.headline: Base Hit! Two-Strike Adjustment Pays Off!
outcome.explanation: Choking up and widening your stance is a classic two-strike or 'I'm struggling' adjustment. Here's why it works: 1) Choking up gives you more bat control because you're holding a shorter, lighter lever. 2) The wider stance lowers your center of gravity, keeping you balanced through the swing. 3) A shorter swing stays in the zone longer, giving you more time to make contact with breaking balls. 4) You gave up some power, but you gained bat control -- and in this situation, contact was more important than power. The best hitters in baseball history -- Tony Gwynn, Ichiro -- shortened up when they were behind or struggling. It's not weakness. It's intelligence.
outcome.whatToRemember: When you're struggling against a pitch, choke up and shorten your swing. You trade power for bat control. Getting a single is better than striking out looking for a home run.
